raw: AGTR1A encodes the angiotensin II type 1a receptor in mice. Loss-of-function of AGTR1A (Agtr1a knockout mice) has been well studied. These mice are viable and fertile. AKT1 is one of three AKT isoforms (AKT1, AKT2, AKT3). In mice, homozygous knockout of AKT1 results in viable animals that are smaller than wild-type (growth retardation) but are generally not reported to die prematurely under normal laboratory conditions. The mice survive to adulthood and reproduce,

APOE knockout mice are viable and fertile. They are well-known for developing hypercholesterolemia and atherosclerosis, but under standard laboratory conditions (normal chow, pathogen-free environment) they do not show dramatically shortened lifespans compared to wild-type mice. ARHGAP1 encodes a Rho GTPase-activating protein (RhoGAP). Based on available literature, ARHGAP1 (also known as CDC42GAP or p50RhoGAP) knockout mice have been studied. The Cdc42GAP (ARHGAP1) knockout mouse model shows that loss of this gene leads to multi-organ defects and premature death. Specifica

ATM (Ataxia Telangiectasia Mutated) is a key DNA damage response kinase. Homozygous ATM knockout mice are well-characterized in the literature. They develop thymic lymphomas at a young age (typically within 2-4 months) and die prematurely due to these malignancies. This is a well-established phenoty

ATR (Ataxia Telangiectasia and Rad3-related) is a critical kinase involved in the DNA damage response, particularly in response to replication stress and single-stranded DNA breaks. BAX is a pro-apoptotic member of the BCL-2 family. Loss of BAX reduces apoptosis, which can lead to accumulation of cells that would normally be eliminated, contributing to tumorigenesis and autoimmune disease over time. BUB1B encodes BUBR1 (also known as MAD3L), a key spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) kinase. Homozygous loss of BUB1B in mice leads to severe mitotic checkpoint deficiency, massive chromosomal instability, and early embryonic lethality or, in hypomorphic models, accelerated aging and premature death.

BUB3 is a spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) gene involved in ensuring proper chromosome segregation during mitosis. In mice, complete loss of BUB3 (homozygous knockout) has been studied. CDKN1A encodes p21 (CIP1/WAF1), a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor involved in cell cycle arrest, senescence, and DNA damage responses. CISD2 (also known as Miner1/NAF-1/WFS2) is a mitochondria-associated gene. DMD encodes dystrophin. In humans, loss of dystrophin causes Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), a severe progressive muscle-wasting disease. However, the mdx mouse (which carries a nonsense mutation in the DMD gene, effectively a loss-of-function) is the classic mouse model of DMD. Notably, mdx mice

ERCC1 is a critical DNA repair gene involved in nucleotide excision repair (NER) and interstrand crosslink repair. ERCC1 knockout mice are well-established in the literature to show severe premature aging phenotypes and die at a young age (typically within a few weeks to months after birth) due to a

ERCC2 (also known as XPD) encodes a DNA helicase that is a component of the TFIIH complex, involved in nucleotide excision repair (NER). In humans, mutations in ERCC2 cause xeroderma pigmentosum, Cockayne syndrome, or trichothiodystrophy, all associated with accelerated aging and reduced lifespan.

FGF23 knockout mice are well-characterized in the literature. FXN encodes frataxin, a mitochondrial protein essential for iron-sulfur cluster assembly. In humans, loss of frataxin causes Friedreich's ataxia, a severe progressive disease. GRN encodes progranulin, a secreted growth factor involved in lysosomal function and neuroinflammation. In mice, homozygous GRN knockout (Grn−/−) mice are viable and fertile but develop progressive neurodegeneration, neuroinflammation, and lipofuscinosis (resembling neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis).

HTRA2 (also known as Omi) is a mitochondrial serine protease involved in apoptosis and mitochondrial quality control. HTT (Huntingtin) is essential for embryonic development. Complete knockout of HTT in mice results in embryonic lethality around embryonic day 7.5-8.5 due to defects in gastrulation. Homozygous Htt knockout mice do not survive to birth, let alone to adulthood.  Answer: A

IGF1 knockout mice are viable and fertile. Importantly, IRS1 knockout mice do not show premature death — in

IRS2 (insulin receptor substrate 2) knockout mice have been studied extensively. The gene KL encodes Klotho, a well-characterized aging-suppressor protein. Homozygous Klotho knockout mice display a severe premature aging syndrome, including growth retardation, infertility, arteriosclerosis, skin atrophy, osteoporosis, and emphysema, and die prematurely at around 8–9 weeks of age

PPARG (PPARγ) homozygous knockout mice die during embryonic development (around E10) due to placental defects (failure of trophoblast differentiation). This constitutes premature death/impaired survival.  Answer: A

PPM1D (also known as Wip1) encodes a phosphatase that negatively regulates the p53/DNA damage response pathway. PPM1D knockout mice have been studied and are viable and fertile. PTEN is a tumor suppressor gene. Homozygous PTEN knockout in mice is embryonic lethal (mice die around embryonic day 6.5-9.5 due to defects in gastrulation and other developmental processes). Therefore, homozygous PTEN knockout mice do not survive to adulthood — they die prematurely (before birth).
